coredata.dc:title: Ammonia-assisted fabrication of Cr1.3Fe0.7O3@amine-containing carbon quantum dot core-shell architectures with ultrathin shell for broad-spectrum-driven photocatalytic hydrogen evolution
coredata.dc:creator: Fu, Zhuquan
Zhang, Piyong
Long, Bei
Ali, Atif
Song, Ting
coredata.dc:description: To make the efficient spatial charge separation and exploration of broad spectrum-driven H2 evolution remains a pivot challenge. Here, a novel core@shell Cr1.3Fe0.7O3@amine-containing carbon quantum dots (S1@NCQD) composite was successfully prepared by a facile bottom-up self-assembly route. An ordinary contact structure of carbon quantum dots (CQD) modified Cr1.3Fe0.7O3 was also prepared. The photocatalytic H2 production rate of Cr1.3Fe0.7O3 composite has not been improved only through the modification of CQD. Remarkably, the as-obtained S1@NCQD composite illustrated the highest photocatalytic H2 production activity under visible light illumination, which is approximately 5.5 and 7.9 times higher than that of S1 and CQD/S1 composite, respectively. Importantly, the new class of S1@NCQD composite can also exploit broad spectrum light to trigger H2 production (3.12 μmol). Effective photoinduced electron transfer is derived from a powerful interaction between shell and core, which was characterized by using photoluminescence, time-resolved PL spectroscopy, and photocurrent measurement.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Charge separation
Hydrogen evolution
Photocatalytic
Ultrathin shell
Core@shell structure

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Science and Technology Innovation Program of Hunan Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Science and Technology Program of Hunan Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We are extremely grateful for the financial support of the Research Fund of the Xiangtan University Startup grant ( 06KZ/KZ08082 ) and the Science and Technology Innovation Program of Hunan Province ( 2020RC2076 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patters of S1, CQD/S1, 1NCQD/S1, N2CQD/S1, and S1@NCQD composites.
Fourier transform infrared spectra of S1, CQD/S1, 1NCQD/S1, N2CQD/S1, and S1@NCQD composites.
XPS analysis of the CQD/S1 and S1@NCQD composites. High resolution XPS spectra of Cr 2p(a), Fe 2p (b), C 1s (c), O 1s (d), and N 1s (e), respectively.

TEM (a) and HRTEM (b) image of S1; HRTEM (c) image of CQD; TEM (d) and HRTEM (e,g) image of CQD/S1; size distribution (f) images of CQD; TEM (m) and HRTEM (n) image of S1@NCQD; EDS spectra (o) and elemental mapping patterns (w) of S1@NCQD.
UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of S1, CQD/S1, 1NCQD/S1, N2CQD/S1, and S1@NCQD composites.

(a) Photocatalytic H2 evolution rates of S1, CQD/S1, 1NCQD/S1, N2CQD/S1, and S1@NCQD composites under visible light irradiation; (b) Photocatalytic activity of the investigated samples in an aqueous solution containing 10 vol% TEOA with added different amounts of carbon quantum dots (CQD) obtained different amounts of citric acid, added different amounts of NH3, or added 2 wt% Pt.
Photocatalytic activity of TiO2, g-C3N4, S1, and S1@NCQD samples in an aqueous solution containing 10 vol% TEOA (irradiation time 4 h) under NIR irradiation (λ > 800 nm).

(a) The cycling test of S1@NCQD sample with no addition sacrificial agent in the photocatalytic experiment period; (b) Steady-state photoluminescence (PL) spectra of S1, CQD/S1, and S1@NCQD; (c) Time-resolved PL decay profiles for S1, CQD/S1, and S1@NCQD; (d) Transient photocurrent responses of S1, CQD/S1, and S1@NCQD in 0.1 mol L−1 Na2SO4 aqueous solution under irradiation with visible light.
A possible mechanism for the improve migration efficiency of photoinduced electron on the photocatalyst interface for S1@NCQD composite.
Schematic illustration of a facile bottom-up self-assembly route to form core@shell structure with ultrathin shell.

head.ce:title: Ammonia-assisted fabrication of Cr1.3Fe0.7O3@amine-containing carbon quantum dot core-shell architectures with ultrathin shell for broad-spectrum-driven photocatalytic hydrogen evolution

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Green Organic Synthesis and Application of Hunan Province, Key Laboratory of Environmentally Friendly Chemistry and Application of Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry, Xiangtan University, Xiangtan 411105, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and State Key Laboratory of Synthetic Chemistry, The University of Hong Kong, Pokfulam Road, Hong Kong, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and State Key Laboratory of Synthetic Chemistry, The University of Hong Kong

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry and State Key Laboratory of Synthetic Chemistry, The University of Hong Kong, Pokfulam Road, Hong Kong, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To make the efficient spatial charge separation and exploration of broad spectrum-driven H2 evolution remains a pivot challenge. Here, a novel core@shell Cr1.3Fe0.7O3@amine-containing carbon quantum dots (S1@NCQD) composite was successfully prepared by a facile bottom-up self-assembly route. An ordinary contact structure of carbon quantum dots (CQD) modified Cr1.3Fe0.7O3 was also prepared. The photocatalytic H2 production rate of Cr1.3Fe0.7O3 composite has not been improved only through the modification of CQD. Remarkably, the as-obtained S1@NCQD composite illustrated the highest photocatalytic H2 production activity under visible light illumination, which is approximately 5.5 and 7.9 times higher than that of S1 and CQD/S1 composite, respectively. Importantly, the new class of S1@NCQD composite can also exploit broad spectrum light to trigger H2 production (3.12 μmol). Effective photoinduced electron transfer is derived from a powerful interaction between shell and core, which was characterized by using photoluminescence, time-resolved PL spectroscopy, and photocurrent measurement.

ce:section.ce:para: Solar energy conversion via suitable photocatalyst is a prospective pathway for clean and renewable energy source [1–3]. Honda and Fujishima first reported photocatalytic water splitting by using TiO2 [4], photocatalytic H2 production has nowadays gained tremendous interest to solve the defect of fossil fuels. Thereafter, various materials suffer from drawbacks of poor visible light capture capability and fast recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, in turn resulting in inefficient hydrogen production activity [5,6]. Compared to several other semiconductor photocatalysts, hematite (α-Fe2O3) has received enormous research attention to date attributed to its chemical stability, low cost, environmental compatibility, natural abundance, and unique properties including suitable band gap (~2.2 eV) [7–9]. However, the fast electron-hole recombination still limits the enhancement of photocatalytic activity for pristine α-Fe2O3 nanoparticles, which significantly hinders its applications [10,11]. Moreover, in photocatalytic H2 evolution, the Cr2O3 nanoparticles have become promising candidates because of their cheaper, low toxicity, and excellent stability [12,13]. However, the Cr2O3 nanoparticles are rarely utilized as a photocatalyst for photocatalytic H2 evolution till now [14]. An effective method of sol-gel can be used to prepare uniform solid solution by the similar ion radius of two metal ions. The process of forming uniform solid solution can effectively regulate the band gap, which is useful for enhancing the photocatalytic activity in the visible light region.

In particular, much attention has been focused on core@shell nanostructured photocatalysts because of enhanced contact area between shell and core, improved electron mobility, and formed a strong electric field at the interface near the band-edge offset [15,16]. Unfortunately, core@shell nanostructured photocatalysts, which is surrounded by thick shells, have negative impact on electron migration [17,18]. Consequently, the key is to prepare ultrathin shells for the purpose of improving the separation and transmission of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Lately, carbon quantum dots (CQD) have fascinated intense interest owing to its abundant, intrinsic low toxicity, chemical stability, and amenability to large-scale and low-cost generation [19–22]. In particular, CQD have achieved improving attention as a multifunctional component for designing catalysts to improve the transmission of photoinduced electron-hole pair [23]. Importantly, the ability of excellent light absorption in CQD endow them to be similar to photosensitizers to capture visible and NIR light, as well as an electronic buffer to provide electrons and migrate electrons in photocatalysts [24,25]. So far, the substrate materials modified with CQD have been constructed, such as CdS [26], WO3 [27], g-C3N4 [28,29], BiVO4 [30], and TiO2 [31,32]. However, the ordinary contact pattern generally results in low conjunction between the bulk solid and CQD, which could greatly hinder the separation and transmission of photoinduced charge [33]. As is known, it is difficult to obtain a core@shell structure for CQD modified bulk materials through the conventional chemical adsorption methods. Therefore, although there are many studies focusing on the CQD modified bulk materials, the fabrication of bulk-materials@CQD core-shell architectures with ultrathin shell and the retention of CQD-related features are still a great challenge.

In this work, we presented the construction and detailed characterization of S1 @NCQD core-shell architectures with ultrathin shell as a new broad-spectrum photocatalyst without precedent. Furthermore, the ordinary contact pattern (CQD/S1) were fabricated through the chemical adsorption method. Meanwhile, a powerful interaction between core and shell led to an ultrafast the separation and migration of photoinduced charge carrier. This synergistic effect also resulted in the production of excellent broad-spectrum-driven photocatalytic activity and stability.
Chromic nitrate, ferric nitrate, citric acid, ethylene glycol, triethanolamine, ethylenediamine, and ammonia were purchased from Aladdin Co. Ltd. The materials were all utilized without purification.

Cr2−xFexO3 nanoparticles were synthesized through a sol-gel method. Firstly, 0.02 mol of Fe(NO3)3 and 0.047 mol of Cr(NO3)3 respectively were added in 11.5 mL Milli-Q ultrapure water (Millipore) under sonication, marked as mixture A. 0.02 mol citric acid was added in 6.7 mL ethane diol under sonication, marked as mixture B. Then, the mixture A was dissolved in the mixture B to obtain the mixture C with vigorous magnetic stirring. The pH of the mixture C was adjusted to 3–4 with ammonia water and then stirring at 70 °C for 4 h to constitute a gel. Next, the gel is added to the oven at 70 °C for 10 h to constitute a xerogel, and then calcined at 900 °C for 1 h. The obtained sample with Fe/Cr molar ratios (3:7) was denoted as S1. Different samples with Fe/Cr molar ratios (1:0, 7:3, 1:1, 3:7, 0:1) were synthesized through the same procedure.

Amine-containing carbon quantum dots (NCQD) were prepared by a hydrothermal method. Generally, 1 g of citric acid was added in 10 mL distilled water. The mixture was delivered to a Teflon autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 6 h. The mixture was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 20 min to eliminate obvious particles after the reactor cooled slowly (2 °C/h) to room temperature. Next, 0.64 mL ammonia was added to the before solution, heated for 6 h at 200 °C in the oven, and then reactor was cooled slowly (2 °C/h) to room temperature. The obtained suspension was further dialyzed via a dialysis (1 kDa, MWCO) for 24 h to remove small particles and nonreacted ions. Finally, the NCQD were isolated by freeze-drying. CQD were synthesized through the above strategy without the addition of ethylenediamine and ammonia. 1NCQD were prepared as follows: citric acid (0.1 g), H2O (10 mL), and ammonia (0.64 mL) were mixed and transferred to a Teflon autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 6 h, and then the Teflon autoclave was cooled slowly (2 °C/h) to room temperature. The purification steps are consistent with the above steps. Detailed synthetic steps for the various materials are displayed in Fig. S1.

xCQD nanoparticles were gathered on the surface of Cr2−xFexO3 nanoparticles through a facile bottom-up self-assembly route. A known amount of xCQD were dispersed in 20 mL Milli-Q ultrapure water under sonication and then added the as-prepared sample (ca. 0.5 g). After stirring for 24 h, the above precursors were dried in oven at 90 °C for 48 h to acquire the resulting sample.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were collected on an electron microscope (TEM-2100F, Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were acquired on a Bruker D8 Focus Diffractometer via using a Cu Kα radiation source. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained by Bruker Tensor 27 spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Axis Ultra DLD, Kratos) was recorded on an ESCALABMKII spectrometer with an Al Kα achromatic X-ray source. The scanning electron microscope (SEM) images were acquired by field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, Hitachi, S-4800). The high-resolution image of the sample is obtained by the transmission electron microscope (TEM-2100F, Japan) characterization, and then the particle size range and the most likely particle size of the sample were calculated by the particle size analysis software (the number of selected samples is more than 30). The UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS, Hitachi U-3010 spectrophotometer) were obtained by the Kubelka-Munk approach with BaSO4 as a reference. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra and transient PL lifetime were investigated on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Instruments, FLSP-920).

The photocatalytic H2 evolution test was carried out on a Labsolar-II AG photocatalytic system (Beijing PerfectLight Company). A certain amount of as-obtained sample was dispersed in 78 mL of aqueous solution containing triethanolamine (10 vol%) as the sacrificial electron donor. Then the aqueous suspension was thoroughly degassed and irradiated with a 300 W Xe lamp (PLSSXE300, Trusttech) equipped with a 420 nm cutoff filter or with a 800 nm cutoff filter. The temperature of the reactant solution was held at room temperature by a flow of cooling water during the reaction. The evolved gases were determined by gas chromatography (GC7900, Tian Mei, Shanghai, TCD detector and 5 Å molecular sieve column) using N2 as the carrier gas.

Electrochemical measurements were conducted with a CHI660E electrochemical workstation in a standard three electrode cell using Ag/AgCl electrode, Pt wire, and sample electrode as the reference electrode, the counter electrode, and working electrode with 0.1 M Na2SO4, respectively. The electrode was prepared as follows: 50 mg of sample powder were dispersed in 1 mL of anhydrous ethanol to produce a slurry. Then, the slurry was coated onto ITO conductive glass using the doctor blade technique.

The critical component of the ultrasonication/self-assembly means is the ammonia at which the carbon quantum dots (CQD) heated to 200 °C for 6 h through hydrothermal reaction. Scheme 1 evidently shows that Cr1.3Fe0.7O3@NCDQ core-shell architectures (S1@NCQD) are obtained by a facile bottom-up self-assembly route, which is obviously distinct from the ordinary contact pattern (CQD/S1). The unique core@shell nanostructured photocatalysts exhibit outstanding broad-spectrum-driven photocatalytic H2 production activity and stability. Moreover, the powerful interaction between shell and core is good for the separation of photoinduced electron-hole at their interface. The optimal contents of citric acid and ammonia for preparing the suitable Cr1.3Fe0.7O3@NCQD are 0.1 g and 0.64 mL, respectively. The structural information of Cr2−xFexO3 composites (Fe/Cr molar ratios of 1:0 (α-Fe2O3), 7:3 (Cr1.2Fe0.8O3/α-Fe2O3), 3:7 (Cr1.3Fe0.7O3), 1:1 (Cr1.2Fe0.8O3), and 0:1 (Cr2O3)) was examined via evaluating their respective XRD patterns (Fig. S3). The similar diffraction peaks of as-obtained composites were attributed to the characteristic peaks of Cr2O3 (JCPDS No. 38-1479, a = b = 4.96, c = 13.59) and the characteristic peaks of α-Fe2O3 (JCPDS No. 33-0664, a = b = 5.03, c = 13.75). The similar diffraction peaks demonstrate that as-obtained samples have the similar lattice constants and same corundum structure. Thus, we succeeded in synthesizing a uniform solid solution without no other impurity components through a sol-gel method. We also investigated the crystal structure of S1, CQD/S1, 1NCQD/S1, N2CQD/S1, and S1@NCQD composites by XRD test ( Fig. 1). The XRD pattern of S1 composite was not affected after xCQD (represent CQD, 1NCQD, N2CQD, and NCQD) modified, indicating the modification of xCQD did not affect the lattice structure of S1. Furthermore, no diffraction peak corresponding to xCQD is observed in as-obtained samples, which may be ascribed to the poor crystalline of xCQD [34].

Fig. 2 displays the FTIR spectra of as-acquired photocatalysts. Fe–O–Cr stretching vibration can lead to the peaks at 544 and 609 cm−1 [35]. For CQD/S1, CQDs exhibit different stretching bands at 3097, 2913, 1718, and 912 cm−1, which respectively are attributed to -OH, C-H, CO, and C–O, confirmed the existence of surface groups such as carbonyl, carboxyl, and hydroxyl on CQD [36,37]. Moreover, the absorption bands at 1100–920 cm−1 are attributed to the CC and C–C groups in CQD, which are ascribed to the photoluminescence of CQD owing to π-π transition [38]. In addition, the peak of C–O and O–H in CQD cannot be observed by reacting with ammonia. Correspondingly, the peaks at 3219 cm−1 and 1209 cm−1 are assigned to the N–H stretching mode and C–N stretching vibration. The bands at 1576 cm−1 and 1484 cm−1 are ascribed to the bending vibration and stretching vibration of -CONH- [39]. These results are consistent with the literature [40]. As a result, the oxygen-containing groups on the surface of CQD react with the ammonia and amine groups are formed in the surface of NCQD.

XPS measurements were studied to determine surface chemical compositions of as-obtained photocatalysts. High-resolution XPS spectra gave Cr 2p, Fe 2p, N 1s, O 1s, and C 1s core electron peaks of CQD/S1 and S1@NCQD materials, respectively. All Cr 2p spectra ( Fig. 3(a)) show characteristic binding energy of 2p1/2 and 2p3/2 at 586.4 and 576.6 eV, which relate very closely with the signals that have been reported [41]. As shown in Fig. 3(b), all Fe 2p spectra show characteristic binding energy of 2p1/2 at 724.2 eV and 2p3/2 at 711.1 eV [42]. The results show that the Cr and Fe species in CQD/S1 and S1@NCQD composites are both trivalence. As shown in Fig. 3(c), the high-resolution spectrum of C 1s can be found four binding energy peaks at 289.0 eV (CO), 286.6 eV (C–O), 285.7 eV (C–N), and 285.0 eV (C–C/CC), respectively, which are agreement with the FTIR results discussed above [43,44]. O 1 s in CQD/S1 (Fig. 3(d)) had two asymmetrical peaks at 532.5 and 529.9 eV, respectively, which could be ascribed to crystal lattice oxygen and the C–OH/C–O [45]. Moreover, the O1s in S1@NCQD can be deconvoluted into two components. On the contrary, the peaks at 531.9 eV and 533.8 eV is attributed to crystal lattice oxygen and chemisorbed oxygen, respectively [46]. The N 1s peaks (Fig. 3(e)) located at 401.6 and 399.8 eV were attributed to N–H and N–C groups, respectively [47]. Therefore, the oxygen-containing groups on the surface of CQD react with the ammonia and amine groups are formed in the surface of NCQD.

The microstructures of as-acquired photocatalysts were characterized by TEM and HRTEM. TEM image shows that the morphological features of S1 composite were irregular nanoparticle ( Fig. 4(a) and S4(a)). The HRTEM image of S1 (Fig. 4(b)) shows that the as-obtained particle is highly crystalline. HRTEM image present lattice spacing of S1 is 0.36 nm, agreeing well with (012) lattice planes of S1. As shown in Fig. 4(c), the HRTEM image illustrated the spherical morphology of CQD without obvious aggregation [48,49]. Fig. 4(f) displays the size distribution of CQDs, disclosed that CQDs are well-dispersed and approximately 1.7 ± 0.9 nm in average particle size with the most possible size of 1.5–1.9 nm. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. S5, NCQD also are monodispersed quasi-spherical particles. However, compared with pure CQD, NCQD showed a unique white halo after ammonia modified. Consistent with the FTIR and XPS results, this shows that the oxygen-containing groups on the surface of CQD react with ammonia to form amine groups on the surface of NCQD.

The morphology of CQD/S1 was tested by TEM (Fig. 4(d) and S4(b)), displaying that the surface of S1 was embedded with distributed nonuniformly and dense dark spots of CQD. As shown in Fig. 4(e) and (g), the crystalline lattice distance of 0.36 nm was attributed to the (012) plane of S1, and the inherent spacing distance of 0.32 nm corresponded to the (002) plane of CQD. Interestingly, the TEM image of S1@NCQD shows the core-shell structure (Fig. 4(m) and S4(c)). It can be seen clearly that the disordered CQD is uniformly coated on the surface of S1 sample under the modification of ammonia, resulting in the formation of NCQD shell. As shown in Fig. 4(n), the outer layer of S1@NCQD sample was obviously different from the S1 core through the HRTEM image. Moreover, the HRTEM image shows that there is an intimate contact between the S1 particles and NCQD ultrathin shell. The thickness of NCQD ultrathin shell is about 3.09 nm. The crystalline lattice distance was detected as d = 0.36 nm, which was attributed to the (012) plane of S1. Compared with the ordinary contact pattern, the S1@NCQD core-shell structure can significantly boost the migration of photoinduced charge carrier duo to it has a powerful interaction. Importantly, the core@shell structure of S1@NCQD was formed by self-assembly process. The EDS characterizations displayed the existence of C, O, Fe, Cr, or N in CQD/S1 (Fig. S6) and S1@NCQD (Fig. 4(o)). The element mapping investigations revealed the presence of C, O, Fe, Cr, or N in CQD/S1 (Fig. S7) and S1@NCQD (Fig. S8 and 4(w)).

The UV–vis DRS was measured to study the optical properties of as-acquired samples. The typical diffuse diffraction spectrum of S1 was recorded in the UV-Vis and NIR regions with a gentle edge in the NIR region ( Fig. 5). Compared with pure S1, the xCQD/S1 materials all exhibited enhanced absorption intensity from 200 to 900 nm. The results of this spectrum display that xCQD can play an important role in absorbing sunlight. The xCQD/S1 composites improve the ability of light absorption to cause more photoinduced charge carrier [50]. This shows that various amine sources and different ammoniation methods to modify CQD do not affect their absorbance properties. Based on the Mott–Schottky plot (Fig. S9) and XPS valence band spectrum (Fig. S10), the conduction band and valence band of S1 was estimated to be −0.88 eV and + 0.85 V (RHE), respectively [51,52].

We evaluated the activity of S1, CQD/S1, 1NCQD/S1, N2CQD/S1 and S1@NCQD for H2 evolution reactions using 10 vol% triethanolamine under visible light illumination ( Fig. 6a). The H2 evolution rates from S1, CQD/S1, S1@NCQD, N2CQD/S1, and 1NCQD/S1 were 4.99, 3.43, 27.22, 0.97, and 3.69 μmol under visible light illumination, respectively. The H2 production rate of S1@NCQD was almost 5.5 and 7.9 times greater than that of S1 and CQD/S1, respectively. The photocatalytic activity of S1 with modified CQD reduces due to excess CQD increased photon absorption and scattering [53]. Therefore, the excellent activities of S1@NCQD were ascribed to the unique core@shell structure that enhanced the migration of photoinduced carrier at their interface. The photocatalytic activities of the NC(x)QD/S1 begins to increase and then gradually decreases as the amount of NCQD introduced increases (Fig. S11 and 6(b)). Among the binary systems, S1@NCQD obtained from the optimal content of citric acid (0.1 g) and ammonia (0.64 mL) displays the optimal H2 production rate, which is approximately 5.5 times higher than that of S1 sample. However, a significantly reduced photocatalytic activity occurs due to the excessive accumulation of NCQD, which would result in restraining the suppression of carrier recombination and shielding active reaction sites [54]. In addition, in order to further improved photocatalytic activity, the different volumes of ammonia modified the optimal CQD were also tested (Fig. S12). The photocatalytic activity of N(x)CQD/S1 composite is improved with increase the volume of ammonia, reached an optimum volume at 0.64 mL and then decreased with further increase the volume of ammonia. The optimal S1@NCQD composite (0.64 mL ammonia) evolved the highest H2 rate. The results indicated that ammonia would further regulate the core@shell structure that enhance the separation of photoinduced charge carrier at their interface. As shown in Fig. S13, the α-Fe2O3, Cr2O3, and g-C3N4 respectively displayed a rate of 3.22, 4.47, and 3.44 μmol. Moreover, the S1@NCQD composite achieves more efficient H2 evolution. To further verify the photocatalytic performance, the H2 production rates of S1 and S1@NCQD samples were extremely higher at NIR light including the average rates of 0.49 μmol and 3.12 μmol ( Fig. 7). Moreover, the photocatalytic activity was negligible in the presence of g-C3N4 and TiO2 samples under NIR irradiation. This phenomenon in the H2 production rates of g-C3N4, TiO2, S1, and S1 @NCQD was ascribed to the different absorbance intensity with different band-gap energy (Fig. S14 and S15). The apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) for S1@NCQD reached 5.1% at 420 nm. After seven consecutive runs, the H2 evolution rate of S1@NCQD is sustained ( Fig. 8(a)). Furthermore, assessment of the FTIR spectra and XRD patterns (Fig. S16) of S1@NCQD illustrates that it indicates good stability [55,56].

PL spectroscopy technique was explored the charge separation and transfer ability of S1, CQD/S1 and S1@NCQD samples (Fig. 8(b)) [57]. It could be observed that the intensity of the emission bands in S1@NCQD was lower than that of S1 and CQD/S1 samples. This result indicates that the recombination of photoinduced charge carrier via the unique core@shell structure with ultrathin shell was more limited for S1 @NCQD than that for S1 and CQD/S1 samples. Meanwhile, the transient fluorescence lifetime (Fig. 8(c)) of S1, CQD/S1, and S1@NCQD samples are respectively 4.35, 4.76, and 3.12 ns via using time-resolved photoluminescence [58]. The decreased decay time of S1@NCQD displayed that there is an efficient migration path, which can more effectively separate the photogenerated charge carrier across the interface between the shell of NCQD and the core of S1. Furthermore, the transient photocurrent response was characterized on the obtained photoelectrode to reveal the charge separation [59,60]. As depicted in Fig. 8(d), the results display that the value of S1@NCQD is significantly larger than that of S1, which reveals the significant increase of the migration rate of charge carriers in S1@NCQD.

Based on the above relevant characterizations, a possible mechanism of S1@NCQD is described in Fig. 9. Under broad-spectrum illumination, S1 is excited to generate electrons in the CB, and its electrons quickly migrate to the surface of CQD to undergo a reduction reaction to produce H2. Meanwhile, the accumulated holes in S1 sample could react with TEOA. Obviously, through adjusting the morphology structure, the unique core@shell structure with ultrathin shell further enhanced the transmission efficiency of photoproduced charge carrier. Compared to the ordinary contact pattern (CQD/S1), the core-shell structure can accelerate the separation of photoinduced charge carrier at their interface. In this process, NCQD are mainly regarded as electron cisterns to trap electrons produced from S1 and restrict charge carrier recombination.

In summary, a cluster of new-designed S1@NCQD core-shell structure with ultrathin shell has been successfully presented here through a facile bottom-up self-assembly route. Compared to the ordinary contact pattern (CQD/S1), the core-shell structure can promote the migration of photoinduced charge carrier at their interface. Among all the discussed photocatalysts above, the S1@NCQD sample possesses the highest photocatalytic activity, which is a outstanding amelioration in contrast to that of pristine S1 and CQD/S1 composite. Hence, the novel S1@NCQD core-shell structure with ultrathin shell furnished a peculiar inspiration for the design and preparation of prospective broad-spectrum photocatalysts to enhance solar energy capture and conversion.

Zhuquan Fu: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Piyong Zhang: Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition. Bei Long: Methodology, Formal analysis. Atif ALi: Visualization, Writing - review & editing. Ting Song: Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are extremely grateful for the financial support of the Research Fund of the Xiangtan University Startup grant (06KZ/KZ08082) and the Science and Technology Innovation Program of Hunan Province (2020RC2076).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2021.161000.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material.
.
